Event: Tormund calls Sandor “the Dog” instead of “the Hound”
Explanation: Familiar teasing slightly amuses himself and mildly annoys Sandor without lasting harm.

Event: Sandor constantly drinks wine and demands wine instead of water during Blackwater
Explanation: Heavy drinking harms his own functioning and reliability while having little clear effect on others.

Event: Sandor abandons his post during the Battle of Blackwater
Explanation: He preserves his own life and sanity at the cost of their military position and trust.

Event: Sandor refuses to become a knight despite qualifying
Explanation: His stance preserves integrity but costs him status and opportunities with no direct benefit to others.

Event: Septon Ray finds Sandor and restores him to health
Explanation: Ray expends effort to save Sandor, gaining a capable ally and benefitting Sandor greatly.

Event: Sandor joins the Brotherhood Without Banners in Season 7
Explanation: He gains belonging and purpose while they gain a powerful fighter.

Event: Sandor defects from the Lannisters
Explanation: Leaving protects him and frees his conscience but undermines their power and security.

Event: Sandor offers to take Arya for ransom to her family
Explanation: He expects payment while also aiming to reunite Arya with her kin.

Event: Sandor goes out of his way to pull Arya out of the Red Wedding massacre
Explanation: He risks himself without ransom prospects to save Arya from certain death.

Event: Sandor helps Arya kill a squad of Frey soldiers
Explanation: He satisfies his own vengeance while helping Arya but further harms their enemies.

Event: Sandor butchers Polliver’s squad partly due to their cruelty
Explanation: He avenges wrongs and removes threats but in a brutal, self-gratifying way.

Event: Sandor resumes killing after Ray’s community is massacred, targeting worse people
Explanation: He channels violence into punishing more dangerous villains while gaining catharsis and purpose.

Event: Sandor protects Sansa from abuse in King’s Landing
Explanation: He risks disfavor with his masters to shield Sansa, gaining some self-respect.

Event: Sandor acts as protector and teacher to Arya while posing as her father
Explanation: He gains companionship and purpose while teaching her survival and defense.

Event: Sandor agrees to bring Arya back for ransom despite mutual hatred
Explanation: He pursues profit by taking on the risk of escorting her to relative safety.

Event: Sandor accepts the nickname “the Hound” and wears a dog helm
Explanation: Embracing a dehumanizing persona aids intimidation but entrenches his own trauma.

Event: Sandor admits his horrible deeds instead of hiding them
Explanation: Acceptance of guilt aids his self-awareness and lets others see him clearly.

Event: Sandor returns to the robbed cottage to bury the devout man and daughter
Explanation: He gains mostly emotional pain and cold while giving them belated dignity and closure.

Event: Sandor repeatedly keeps Arya safe physically and mentally over the years
Explanation: Acting as a guardian gives him purpose and gives Arya survival and emotional support.

Event: At the Red Keep, Sandor convinces Arya to abandon revenge and leave
Explanation: He knowingly throws away his own chance to live while saving Arya from likely death.

Event: Arya thanks Sandor and calls him by his true name as they part
Explanation: Her gratitude gives him emotional closure while honoring what he did.

Event: Sandor fights Gregor to a standstill and obeys King Robert’s order to stop
Explanation: He proves his prowess and loyalty while preventing Gregor from murdering Loras.

Event: Sandor guts Sansa’s would-be rapists during a riot and rescues her
Explanation: He risks himself in chaos to save Sansa and decisively stop multiple attackers.

Event: Sandor cuts down Stannis’ men during Blackwater
Explanation: He performs as paid killer, boosting his standing while harming the attackers.

Event: Sandor kills a squad of Frey men in the S3 finale
Explanation: He gains revenge and safety for himself and Arya at the expense of enemy troops.

Event: Sandor fights Brienne, is badly wounded, and asks Arya to mercy-kill him
Explanation: His stubborn combat nearly kills him and his plea gives Arya a difficult choice without aiding others.

Event: Arya robs the wounded Sandor and walks away without killing him
Explanation: She gains resources and emotional satisfaction while leaving him in agony and probable death.

Event: Sandor repeatedly tucks into food taken from those he’s killed or beaten
Explanation: He nourishes himself at the direct expense of the people he’s just harmed.

Event: Sandor saves Sansa from attempted gang rape in “The Old Gods and the New”
Explanation: He risks himself in a riot to rescue her from severe harm with no clear downside.

Event: Sandor abandons Joffrey during the riot to search for and rescue Sansa
Explanation: He forsakes his formal duty to protect the person actually in mortal danger.

Event: Sandor and Beric save Arya from wights; Sandor carries her to safety
Explanation: He risks death in the Long Night to pull Arya out of overwhelming danger.

Event: Sandor demands wine after seeing a man burn at Blackwater
Explanation: He self-medicates trauma in a way that doesn’t help him long-term.

Event: Sandor joins the Kingsguard while refusing knighthood
Explanation: He gains elite status and security while enforcing a tyrannical regime.

Event: Sandor stands by while Tyrion slaps Joffrey without intervening
Explanation: He avoids conflict, subtly undercuts Joffrey, and nothing bad comes of his inaction.

Event: Sandor kills Mycah the butcher’s boy on Joffrey’s command
Explanation: Obeying gains him favor with his masters at the cost of murdering an innocent child.

Event: Sandor obeys Ned’s order and blocks Gregor from killing Loras
Explanation: He curbs Gregor’s rampage, earning royal approval and saving Loras’ life.

Event: Sandor refuses to steal Joffrey’s goods when fleeing, citing a code
Explanation: He preserves his self-image and avoids extra risk without clear cost or gain to others.

Event: Sandor robs the farmer and daughter who sheltered him and Arya
Explanation: He secures money for himself but dooms their already fragile household.

Event: Sandor leaves the robbed farmer and daughter alive
Explanation: He spares them, which later means they suffer and starve rather than quick death.

Event: Sandor later finds the farmer and daughter dead and buries them
Explanation: He gains mostly guilt and hardship while giving them respect in death.

Event: Sandor threatens Polliver and ultimately slaughters him and his men
Explanation: He avenges past wrongs and gets resources by brutally eliminating them.

Event: Sandor deserts the defenders during Blackwater saying “Fuck the King”
Explanation: He saves himself and exits a traumatizing battle but abandons his sworn side.

Event: Sandor testifies truthfully to the Brotherhood about his own crimes
Explanation: Honest admission helps fair judgment and marks a step toward self-awareness.

Event: Sandor helps Arya flee when she kills a Frey soldier in front of others
Explanation: He quickly kills the witnesses and extricates her from a lethal situation.

Event: Sandor uses the Hound persona to cope with what he’s done
Explanation: The mask shields him in the short term but deepens alienation and unresolved trauma.

Event: Sandor explodes at Brienne about “no safety” and refuses to give Arya up
Explanation: His trauma-driven outburst worsens his odds yet is motivated by concern for Arya.

Event: Sandor runs in and fights off Gregor at the tourney melee
Explanation: He stops Gregor from murdering Loras and displays loyalty and skill.

Event: Sandor threatens men to take their tavern table and beer
Explanation: He asserts dominance and gets comfort while intimidating and displacing others.

Event: Sandor trains Arya by punching her during Water Dancing practice
Explanation: His harsh demonstration risks minor harm but effectively teaches real-world combat limits.

Event: Sandor murders Stark guards in the throne room under Slynt’s order
Explanation: He secures his standing as an enforcer by butchering loyal men.

Event: Sandor taunts Arya after his acquittal over Mycah’s death
Explanation: He amuses himself emotionally while deepening her pain and hatred.

Event: Sandor beats up a man and steals his valuables instead of killing him
Explanation: He enriches himself while sparing the peasant’s life yet likely worsening his fate.

Event: Sandor defends Sansa repeatedly from Joffrey’s sadism
Explanation: He takes personal risk to blunt the king’s cruelty toward her.

Event: Sandor spares Joffrey’s property when fleeing because “a man’s got to have a code”
Explanation: He keeps his personal code intact without materially changing anyone’s situation.

Event: Sandor accepts work and peace in Septon Ray’s community
Explanation: Physical labor and community give him healing while the village gains his strength.

Event: Sandor goes on a revenge hunt for Septon Ray’s murderers
Explanation: He gains emotional satisfaction eliminating killers but acts from personal vengeance.

Event: Sandor executes Lem and another man by hanging with Brotherhood’s consent
Explanation: He gets partial revenge through relatively clean execution of guilty men.

Event: Sandor kills extra rogue outlaws on his own using extreme brutality
Explanation: He indulges in overkill while still removing dangerous murderers.

Event: Sandor joins the Brotherhood on their wight-hunting mission
Explanation: He risks his life for a cause that protects the entire realm and finds a role.

Event: Sandor uses his tracking skills to find targets (e.g., Ray’s killers)
Explanation: His tracking serves his ends, usually to capture or kill others.

Event: Sandor euthanizes a dying merchant to end his suffering
Explanation: He shoulders moral weight to give the man a quick, merciful death.

Event: Sandor overcomes panic at Long Night upon seeing Arya in danger
Explanation: Renewed resolve helps him save her and resume fighting.

Event: Sandor comforts Beric when Thoros dies
Explanation: He gains some human connection while easing another’s grief.

Event: Sandor later beams with pride hearing how capable Arya has become
Explanation: He gains satisfaction and validation in seeing his “pupil” thrive.

Event: Sandor tells Arya that vengeance will leave her hateful and alone
Explanation: He gains little besides honesty while strongly steering her away from his own ruinous path.

Event: Sandor chooses to go to King’s Landing to kill Gregor despite the danger
Explanation: His quest for revenge gives meaning but leads almost certainly to his death.

Event: Sandor tackles Gregor through a wall into dragonfire, killing them both
Explanation: He sacrifices his own life to finally destroy his brother, ending a major threat at fatal cost.

Event: Sandor refuses knighthood due to disgust at the institution
Explanation: He preserves integrity yet forgoes power that could have been used for good.

Event: Sandor says “Fuck Joffrey, fuck the Queen” and makes clear he’s out
Explanation: He declares freedom from a toxic allegiance while weakening their hold.

Event: Sandor uses Septon Ray’s community for Percussive Therapy labor
Explanation: Hard work helps process trauma while contributing needed strength to the villagers.

Event: Sandor warns Arya to at least tell him next time she pulls a revenge kill
Explanation: He normalizes her vengeance but encourages coordination for mutual safety.

Event: Sandor expresses pride and laughter at Sansa feeding Ramsay to his hounds
Explanation: He validates her strength and gives her emotional support for reclaiming power.

Event: Sandor shows restraint by not torturing the three condemned rogue outlaws
Explanation: He surrenders his urge for cruelty and keeps the executions relatively humane.

Event: Sandor offers Sansa escape from King’s Landing during Blackwater
Explanation: He risks Joffrey’s wrath to give her a chance at survival and freedom.

Event: Sandor refuses to be thanked for saving Sansa
Explanation: He declines emotional reward while letting her keep dignity without obligation.

Event: Sandor fights Brienne to keep Arya with him
Explanation: His misjudgment leads to grave injury without benefiting Arya, who survives regardless.

Event: Sandor refuses Arya’s attempt to cauterize his infected bite wound
Explanation: His stubbornness worsens his condition and weakens him before fighting Brienne.

Event: Sandor later reconciles with Arya when they meet at Winterfell
Explanation: Mutual forgiveness eases old wounds and restores a supportive bond.

Event: Sandor threatens polliver’s men about “eating every chicken in this room”
Explanation: He asserts dominance and sets up a fight where he’ll kill them for his and Arya’s gain.

Event: Sandor chooses quick kills instead of torture for most enemies
Explanation: He still kills but deliberately avoids unnecessary suffering.

Event: Sandor repeatedly uses snark and insults as a coping mechanism
Explanation: His hostility protects him emotionally but pushes others away.

Event: Sandor admits his fear of fire (e.g., explaining his scars to Arya)
Explanation: Vulnerability deepens their bond and helps Arya understand him.

Event: Sandor rejects naïve optimism, trying to prepare Arya for Westeros
Explanation: His harsh lessons increase her survival skills even if they hurt her feelings.

Event: Sandor punches Arya unconscious when taking her from the Brotherhood
Explanation: He secures his ransom chance at the cost of harming and overriding her will.

Event: Sandor arranges to ransom Arya despite knowing she wants him dead
Explanation: He pursues profit while realistically planning to reunite her with her kin.

Event: Sandor carries boots and supplies off the hanged outlaw he executes
Explanation: He uses a dead man’s gear for practical gain with minimal extra harm.
